entries.name: Pattern A: encrypted-VM bytecode interpreter (Unity IL2CPP target)
aliases: Unity IL2CPP with custom VM layer

entries.name: Pattern A-VMT: encrypted-VM handler-table dispatch (BlackSpace engine)

defender.summary: Dual-regime .xcode section (low-entropy dispatch table + high-entropy encrypted metadata) + statically-compiled handler bodies in .arch + runtime-decrypted handler targets in .link. The Pearl Abyss BlackSpace engine's signature.

detection_signatures.v0_7_0_note: Replaces the v0.6.0 loose-intersect signature that fired on UE4 binaries missing .link. The 0.6.0 signature is kept below for backward compat.

false_positive_risks: v0.6.0 known issue: the loose `section_set_intersects([.arch, .link, .xcode, .xtext, .sbss])` signature fires on UE4 binaries that lack `.link` (e.g., P3R matched Pattern A-VMT at 0.95 despite being Unreal Engine 4, not BlackSpace). v0.7.0 adds the stricter `section_set_contains_all_of([.arch, .link, .xcode, .sbss])` signature. P3R no longer matches.
Very few false positives. The dual-regime + POGO + ILTCG combo is highly specific to BlackSpace (when the strict signature is used).

offender.summary: Read the .xcode dispatch table (16-byte big-endian entries [u32 id][u32 reserved=0][u64 target]); resolve the handler targets in .link (BSS); lift the handler bodies in .arch. Reconstruct the full handler table.
limitations: Handler bodies in .arch are static x86_64 — these are the easy part. The hard part is the runtime-decrypted handler targets in .link, which require runtime hooks.

references.note: Pattern A-VMT was added to the taxonomy in v2.9.0 (2026-06-07).

entries.name: Pattern B: hardware-fingerprinting + anti-debug in third-party launcher activation library

defender.summary: Small (1-3 MB) DLL with ordinal-only exports; launcher imports only 2-3 ordinals; statically-linked crypto; WinHTTP + X.509 / Authenticode imports; 8+/12 HWID APIs; anti-debug split between activation DLL and game DLL.

limitations: The stub-drop only removes the activation check. The game still validates the activation on a periodic basis. Online entitlement re-check may still reject.

references.note: LIR's Core/Activation64.dll is the canonical Pattern B example.

entries.name: Pattern D: publisher telemetry pipeline leak (attack surface)
aliases: Sentry / Logstash / Confluence / Drive / AWS / Slack leak

defender.summary: Sentry DSN, Logstash URL, Confluence wiki, Google Drive, AWS access keys, or Slack tokens in the binary's string table. Often indicates the binary was built against an internal corporate resolver and shipped without scrubbing.

detection_signatures.note: Dot does not need escaping in YARA string literals (post-v0.5.0 migration from `\.` to `.` — YARA 4.x rejects `\.` as illegal escape in string literals).

false_positive_risks: Low. These are highly specific patterns; false-positive rate is < 1%.

offender.summary: Document the leak. The leak is itself the attack surface — you can use it to find related infrastructure, additional internal docs, or build a targeted social-engineering pretext. The leak is also useful for defensive triage: it tells you which publisher's internal network the binary was built against.
limitations: The leak is read-only. You can't decrypt or bypass anything; you just have a URL to a publisher's internal wiki.

aliases: PDB-path tells you the project name

aliases: Epic Online Services dev-mode escape hatch

defender.summary: The EOS overlay (EOSSDK-Win64-Shipping.dll, 19MB) is loaded as a sibling DLL. The overlay checks EOS entitlement at launch via EOS_Platform_TryInitialize. The bypass is a documented dev-mode escape hatch: drop a .eos side-file.

defender.summary: Reads the Time Stamp Counter twice and measures the delta. If the delta exceeds a threshold (typically 100-1000 cycles), the code assumes a debugger or VM is single-stepping.
false_positive_risks: Performance-counter usage is also legitimate. Confirm with the RDTSC-pair pattern.

offender.summary: Two strategies: (a) hook the RDTSC instruction to return a constant value (0x1000 = 'always 4096 cycles, regardless of actual TSC'); (b) at static-patch time, replace each `rdtsc` opcode (`0F 31`) with a `xor eax, eax; xor edx, edx` (8 bytes total: `33 C0 33 D2`). Strategy (b) is per-binary but reliable; strategy (a) is reusable.
limitations: Per-binary patching; the strategy needs to be re-applied per build.

defender.summary: Executes INT 0x2d. Under a kernel debugger, this triggers the debugger to gain control. Under a user-mode debugger (or no debugger), INT 0x2d is a no-op or raises EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT.
false_positive_risks: Very low. INT 0x2d has no legitimate use in user-mode code.

offender.summary: NOP-out the INT 0x2d (replace 2 bytes `CD 2D` with `90 90` = two NOPs). Or hook the exception handler to ignore EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT.
limitations: None.

defender.summary: Executes INT 3 (0xCC). Under a debugger, this triggers the debugger to gain control. As padding, 0xCC is the canonical x86 `int3` instruction used to fill unused space.
false_positive_risks: VERY high. 0xCC is everywhere in x86 binaries as padding. Per-function disassembly is required to distinguish padding from anti-debug trap.

offender.summary: If a function entry-point or specific control-flow path has 0xCC, the function is a trap. Replace the 0xCC with 0x90 (NOP) and the function continues normally. If 0xCC is in a tail-padding region (post-RET), it's just padding — no action needed.
limitations: Requires per-function disassembly to identify which 0xCC bytes are traps vs padding. 0xCC is the most ambiguous primitive in x86.

entries.name: CPUID leaf 1 ECX bit 31 (hypervisor present)
aliases: 0F A2 opcode followed by bt ecx, 0x1F

defender.summary: Executes CPUID with EAX=1, then tests bit 31 of ECX. If set, the CPU is running under a hypervisor. Used to detect VM/sandbox environments.
false_positive_risks: CPUID is used legitimately for many purposes. Confirm with the bit-31-test pattern.

offender.summary: Hook CPUID to return 0 for bit 31 of ECX when EAX=1. The 'bare-metal' CPUID snapshot is the source of the spoofed value.
limitations: Some protectors also check the timing of CPUID (CPUID should take ~100 cycles; under VM it can take 1000+). Combine with the RDTSC timing-trap bypass for full coverage.

defender.summary: Attempts to execute VMXON (Intel VT-x virtualization). If the CPU supports VT-x and VMXON is not already in use, the CPU enters VMX operation. Used to detect the presence of a hypervisor and to fingerprint Intel VT-x capabilities.
false_positive_risks: 0F C7 is also the CMPXCHG16B opcode. Confirm with VMCS-region setup code.

offender.summary: Hook the VMXON instruction to return #UD (undefined opcode). The binary's VMXON probe then sees 'VT-x not available' and treats the environment as bare-metal.
limitations: VMXON can also be detected via CPUID VMX feature bits. Combine with the leaf-1-ECX-bit-31 bypass.

defender.summary: Executes VMCALL (0F 01 C1). If the CPU is in VMX non-root operation, VMCALL traps to the hypervisor. If the hypervisor doesn't handle the call, the CPU injects #UD. The probe is used to detect the presence of a hypervisor (and to fingerprint it via the hypercall number).
false_positive_risks: Low. VMCALL has no legitimate use in user-mode code outside of VMX-aware tools.

offender.summary: Hook VMCALL to return without trapping. Or use CPUID feature-bit masking to disable VMX (less common; most CPUs have VMX forced-on).
limitations: Some protectors also probe VMCS magic numbers via RDMSR.

defender.summary: Executes INVD (invalidate caches, no flush). Real CPUs handle this in microcode. Some emulators (Speakeasy, QEMU without TCG acceleration, Unicorn) crash or behave incorrectly. Used to detect emulated execution.
false_positive_risks: INVD has no legitimate use in user-mode code; if present, it's anti-emulator. False-positive rate is <5%.

offender.summary: Hook INVD to be a NOP (or a benign no-op). For emulators: enable TCG acceleration or handle the INVD instruction in the emulator. For Frida: replace `INVD` with `NOP; NOP` (2 bytes).
limitations: If the emulator supports INVD natively (e.g. Speakeasy 1.5.11 does), no action is needed.

defender.summary: Reads the PEB.BeingDebugged byte (offset 0x02 in the PEB on x64, 0x02 on x86). When the process is being debugged, this byte is 1. IsDebuggerPresent() is the canonical API that wraps this read.

detection_signatures.note: mov rax, gs:[0x60] = read PEB on x64

false_positive_risks: Low. The IsDebuggerPresent string is almost always present in any Windows binary, including legitimate ones. Confirm with the actual read of PEB.BeingDebugged.

offender.summary: Patch the PEB.BeingDebugged byte to 0. Or hook the IsDebuggerPresent function. Or use a tool like ScyllaHide (x64dbg plugin) to hide from PEB.BeingDebugged at debug-time.
limitations: The protector may also check NtQueryInformationProcess(ProcessDebugPort), which is a different probe. Combine with that bypass for full coverage.

defender.summary: Reads 5-10 registry keys (HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion, HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Cryptography, etc.) and concatenates their values to form a hardware fingerprint. VMs often have predictable registry values.
false_positive_risks: Registry reads are very common. Confirm with the entropy-concatenation pattern.

offender.summary: Hook the registry read functions to return a 'bare-metal' snapshot of the registry values. Alternatively, set the registry to the right values at the host level (persistent).
limitations: The list of probed keys is per-binary; you need to identify the probed keys first.

entries.name: Network adapter MAC prefix (VM detection via MAC OUI)

defender.summary: Reads the network adapters' MAC addresses and checks the OUI (organizationally unique identifier) prefix against known VM vendors. Common VM OUIs: 00:0C:29 (VMware), 08:00:27 (VirtualBox), 00:1C:42 (Parallels), 00:50:56 (VMware again).
false_positive_risks: GetAdaptersAddresses is used legitimately for many purposes. Confirm with the OUI-check pattern.

offender.summary: At the host level, set the network adapter's MAC to a bare-metal OUI (e.g. an Intel OUI like 00:1B:21, 00:1D:E0, 00:23:6C, 00:24:D6, 00:26:5A, 00:26:C6, 00:26:F0, etc.). Alternatively, hook GetAdaptersAddresses at runtime to return the spoofed MAC.
limitations: Host-level MAC set is the most reliable; runtime hook is a fallback.

defender.summary: Communicates with the VMware hypervisor via the magic I/O port 0x5658. IN EAX, 0x5658 with EAX='VX' (0x56415858) returns 'VMw' (0x564D7772) under VMware. This is the most reliable VMware detection.

detection_signatures.note: mov ax, 0x5658
in al, dx

false_positive_risks: Low. Port 0x5658 reads are not used for legitimate purposes in user-mode code.

offender.summary: Hook the I/O port reads. Speakeasy and QEMU can intercept IN instructions; for native execution, use a hypervisor (HyperHide) or a custom kernel driver. Easiest: at the host level, change the VMware backdoor port or use HyperHide.
limitations: Requires a kernel-mode handler. User-mode-only approaches are incomplete.

entries.name: MBA identity: x + y = (x XOR y) + 2*(x AND y)
aliases: x + y = (x XOR y) + 2*(x AND y)

defender.summary: The identity x + y = (x XOR y) + 2*(x AND y) is the canonical MBA identity. Detected by finding the pattern of XOR + AND + LSL1 + ADD instructions.

detection_signatures.note: xor eax, eax; and eax, ecx
xor eax, ecx; shl eax, 1

false_positive_risks: The patterns are common in optimized code. Confirm with the algebraic structure.

offender.summary: Use re-mba-deobfuscate skill or re-triton to symbolically simplify the expression. The pattern collapses back to `x + y` after simplification.
limitations: Some MBA identities are intentionally added to thwart symbolic execution. The Triton + Z3 constraint solver should still converge.

entries.name: Opaque predicate: (x XOR x) is always 0

defender.summary: An opaque predicate is an expression that always evaluates to a constant at runtime, but cannot be simplified at compile-time. The canonical example is (x XOR x) = 0 — but with MBA obfuscation, the predicate can be much more complex.

detection_signatures.note: xor eax, eax

false_positive_risks: XOR reg, reg is used legitimately to zero a register. Confirm with the predicate usage pattern (always followed by a conditional branch).

offender.summary: Use re-triton or re-angr with symbolic execution to evaluate the predicate symbolically. If it always evaluates to 0 (or always to 1), the predicate is opaque and the conditional branch can be removed.
limitations: Some opaque predicates are intentionally complex; symbolic execution may not converge in reasonable time.

defender.summary: OLLVM (Obfuscator-LLVM) is the most widely deployed open-source obfuscator. Its 'control-flow flattening' pass transforms structured control flow into a single switch statement with an opaque-predicate-driven state variable. Combined with MBA, this is the strongest open-source obfuscation pattern.
false_positive_risks: Some legitimate interpreters use a state machine. Confirm with the MBA + opaque-predicate combination.

offender.summary: Use re-vtil's control-flow-unflatten pass (with the OLLVM catalog pass set) or re-angr's CFG recovery. The switch + state variable can be unflattened back to structured if/else/while.
limitations: OLLVM's control-flow flattening can be combined with virtualization (FLA + VIR), which makes the unflatten + VIR-lift pipeline much harder.

entries.name: Denuvo Anti-Tamper (Denuvo Anti-Cheat is a separate product)

defender.summary: Denuvo is a commercial anti-tamper solution used by major AAA game titles. It uses a combination of encrypted-VM bytecode interpreter + POGO-based profile-guided-optimization triggers + online license validation. Per the v2.9.0 stress test, the canonical example is Persona 3 Reload (P3R.exe, 373MB, 19 sections).

detection_signatures.note: The string 'denuvo' typically appears in the binary (often in plaintext, sometimes in encrypted sections).

false_positive_risks: Low. POGO + .arch is a very specific signature.

offender.summary: Months of per-title work. Denuvo is the hardest commercial ATD; there is no public bypass. The realistic approaches are: (a) dump decrypted regions + bypass the online license check (still requires the binary to phone home to Denuvo's server); (b) emulate the binary in a sandbox where the RDTSC trap is neutralized (the Denuvo VFS traps RDTSC anomalies with high confidence); (c) wait for Denuvo to retire the entitlement (publishers do this when they deprecate the game).
limitations: Denuvo Anti-Tamper is the hardest commercial protection. No public bypass exists.

references.note: The P3R per-game doc (the canonical Denuvo ATD case).
P3R stress test (3 'denuvo' string hits + full A-DW section set).

defender.summary: VMProtect is a commercial protector that uses an encrypted-VM bytecode interpreter to protect native code. VMP 3.x is the current major version. VMP-protected binaries have a distinct section layout (.vmp0, .vmp1) and a dispatcher that decrypts and executes one VM handler at a time.

detection_signatures.note: VMP section name.
VMP version string.

false_positive_risks: Very low. VMP section names are highly specific.

offender.summary: The VMP open-source community has produced several tools: `void-stack/VMUnprotect`, `can1357/NoVmp`, `wallds/NoVmpy`, `fjqisba/VmpHelper`, `CabboShiba/VMPBypass`, `chramiq/de4vmp`, `Shhoya/MutantKiller`, `r3bb1t/vmp_analyzer`, `crowbar-team/vmp-import-resolver`, `nelj14/vmprotect-dumper`, `Thiviyan/VMProfiler-QT`, `keowu/birosca`. The approach: hook VMP's dispatcher, dump each VM handler as it executes.
limitations: Per-build breakable. VMP 3.5+ uses rolling keys + custom handlers. The open-source tools are most effective against VMP 3.0-3.4.

defender.summary: Intel SGX (Software Guard Extensions) is a TEE that allows an application to execute code in a hardware-isolated 'enclave'. The enclave's contents are encrypted in DRAM; even the OS cannot read them. Used by some DRM + financial-services apps.

detection_signatures.note: sgx_* API prefixes.

false_positive_risks: Low. SGX enclaves are explicit in the binary.

offender.summary: SGX enclaves are designed to be unbreakable from outside. The realistic approaches: (a) side-channel attacks (e.g. Spectre, Foreshadow); (b) fault injection (e.g. voltage glitching); (c) extract the enclave's sealing key from the platform. None are software-only.
limitations: SGX is a hardware-isolated TEE. Software-only approaches are ineffective. Side-channel + fault-injection are the only paths.

defender.summary: The original code is converted to bytecode for a custom VM. The VM is then compiled into the protected binary. The VM's dispatcher interprets the bytecode one instruction at a time.
false_positive_risks: Some legitimate interpreters use a similar pattern. Confirm with the encryption of the dispatch table.

offender.summary: Same as for any encrypted-VM bytecode interpreter: hook the dispatcher, dump each VM handler as it executes, lift to readable IL.
limitations: Per-VM. The VM's specific handler semantics must be understood.

entries.name: String encryption: XOR with constant key

defender.summary: String literals in the binary are XOR-encrypted with a constant key. The key is typically embedded in the binary as an immediate value or a small array of immediates.
false_positive_risks: Many legitimate binaries use XOR for their own data.

offender.summary: Extract the XOR key from the binary (one immediate value or a small array). Run the XOR over the entire string table. Optionally use re-llm-decompile to feed the deobfuscated function to an LLM for explanation.
limitations: None significant.

entries.name: String encryption: AES (or other block cipher)

defender.summary: String literals are AES-encrypted (typically AES-CBC or AES-CTR with a key derived from a constant + the binary's entropy). The decryption function is in the binary; calling it on a single encrypted block returns the plaintext.
false_positive_risks: AES is used legitimately in many places. Confirm with the string-table decryption pattern.

offender.summary: Either (a) extract the AES key + IV from the binary, decrypt the string table directly; or (b) call the binary's own decryption function on each encrypted block. The Frida + RE approach: hook the decryption function, decrypt each string at runtime, log to a file.
limitations: Some protectors use a per-string key derived from the binary's hash; the key extraction is per-target.

defender.summary: The binary does not import the Win32 APIs directly. Instead, it computes the API name's hash at runtime, walks the kernel32.dll / ntdll.dll export table, and matches the hash. This hides the API names from static analysis tools (the IAT is empty).
false_positive_risks: Some legitimate shellcode uses this pattern.

offender.summary: Hook the API-resolution function (the function that walks the export table). Log each resolved API name to a file. The re-frida approach: Interceptor.attach on the resolution function, capture arguments + return value.
limitations: Some protectors use a per-build hash; the resolution function must be identified per binary.

defender.summary: Pattern C proprietary engines (IOI Glacier, Pearl Abyss BlackSpace) use switch-based control-flow flattening. A central dispatcher reads a state variable from a register, jumps to the corresponding basic block, each block updates the state variable, and the loop continues.
false_positive_risks: JMP [reg*4+disp32] is also used in legitimate code (jump tables). The Pattern C variant is identified by the dispatcher reading a state register and the basic blocks writing back to the same register.

offender.summary: Use the in-tree re-vm-decrypt + re-encrypted-vm-bypass to lift the dispatcher + each basic block. Reconstruct the original control-flow from the lifted bytecode.riginal control flow by inverting the CFF: collapse the dispatcher + the state-variable update into a direct basic-block-to-basic-block edge. Per Pattern C, the dispatcher is also wrapped in the encrypted-VM bytecode interpreter — first lift the encrypted methods via re-vm-decrypt, then deflatten each method.
limitations: CFF recovery is heuristic; manual verification is required for each method
Some Pattern C variants use opaque predicates to obfuscate the state-variable update, which makes the inversion ambiguous

defender.summary: Pattern C proprietary engines replace IAT imports with custom hash lookups. Each Win32 API is referenced by a 32-bit hash (typically MurmurHash3 or FNV-1a) computed at build time. The hash lookup is done at runtime via a custom resolver that walks the PEB_LDR_DATA and hashes each export name until it matches.
false_positive_risks: The shift-left pattern (C1 E0 02) is common in many code paths; the PEB_LDR_DATA reference is a stronger signal but is also used in non-obfuscated code.

offender.summary: Build a hash-to-API map for the target by computing the same hash function over each Win32 API name. Replace the custom resolver with a direct IAT lookup. Use the in-tree vendored's re-lief to extract the import names + the in-tree vendored's re-symbolic-exec to enumerate the hash function.
limitations: The hash function may be per-binary (rotated at each build), requiring per-build re-computation
Some imports are resolved via direct syscalls instead of the Win32 API, bypassing the resolver entirely

entries.name: Timing-trap detection (Pattern A-DW Denuvo ATD variant)

defender.summary: Pattern A-DW uses a bimodal distribution of RDTSC deltas to detect virtualization. The target calls RDTSC twice with a sensitive API in between; if the delta is bimodal (one cluster of fast, one cluster of slow), the target detects the hypervisor trap. The Denuvo ATD variant uses 200+ of these timing-trap sites.
false_positive_risks: 200+ RDTSC sites is also a fingerprint of high-performance code; the discriminator is the bimodal delta distribution, not the raw count.

offender.summary: Cap the RDTSC delta to a configurable threshold (default 1000 cycles). Any delta > cap returns the cap value, defeating the bimodal distribution. Use the in-tree vendored's re-frida + a CPUID/RDTSC hook with delta capping. Per SOW-A, the Bypass technique is the deliverable; the cap value is reported to Denuvo only.
limitations: Per MRTEA Part V §4.1, no key extraction as a deliverable
Per MRTEA SOW-A, per-title Denuvo bypass is months of work; this is a class-of-technique Bypass, not a per-title tool
Denuvo may detect the RDTSC cap and escalate to a different detection path

defender.summary: InsaneRamZes-style cracks (and modern protectors like Vanguard-class AC) probe for the presence of VM-related drivers and devices: C:\Windows\System32\drivers\vboxguest.sys, vmci.sys, vboxmouse.sys, vboxsf.sys, vmusbmouse.sys, vmhgfs.sys, and the device paths \\.\VBoxGuest, \\.\VMCI, \\.\VMware. Module-name probe: VBoxHook.dll, vmtools.dll. The probe is typically a string-literal table that the code iterates at runtime via GetModuleHandle/CreateFileW.

detection_signatures.note: vmci is short — pair with one other signature to reduce FPs
Dot does not need escaping in YARA string literals (post-v0.5.0 migration from `\.` to `.` — YARA 4.x rejects `\.` as illegal escape in string literals).

Dot does not need escaping in YARA string literals (post-v0.5.0 migration from `\.` to `.` — YARA 4.x rejects `\.` as illegal escape in string literals).

false_positive_risks: Modules that legitimately load VBoxHook.dll (VBox guest tools) — but those are the guest, not the host probe. Pair with anti-vm.process-module-name-enumeration for higher confidence.

offender.summary: Strip the driver-name table from the binary (in-source enumeration). For runtime probes (CreateFileW on \\.\VBoxGuest), use re-anti-vm-spoof's frida hook to redirect the open to a fake device that returns ERROR_FILE_NOT_FOUND. For driver load checks, hide the driver behind a custom service that returns STATUS_OBJECT_NAME_NOT_FOUND.
limitations: The probe is sometimes a CRC-checked table — patching requires CRC recompute.
The driver may be loaded as a hidden filter driver that bypasses standard enumeration.

references.note: F1 25 InsaneRamZes crack — driver probe is part of the 23 VMCALL + 2.18M CPUID layer.

defender.summary: Execute SIDT/SGDT/SLDT/STR in user-mode; the IDT/GDT base on a hypervisor is at a known offset from a known host value (Peter Ferrie's red-pill). On bare metal, the IDT is at 0x80xxxx. On a hypervisor (VBox, VMware, KVM), it's at 0xFFxxxx. Pairing with a CPUID check (CPUID.1:ECX[31] = 1 -> running under hypervisor) is the standard red-pill + no-pill combo. Catches hypervisors that strip the CPUID hypervisor-present bit but forget to scrub the descriptor tables.

detection_signatures.note: SGDT [mem]
SIDT [mem]
SLDT [mem] (STR mem variant also exists: 0F 00 1D)
STR [mem]
CPUID -- pair with one of the above descriptor-table opcodes to filter out kernel-mode use; kernel-mode IDT accesses are normal

false_positive_risks: Kernel-mode code uses SIDT/SGDT legitimately. Filter to user-mode user32/kernel32 callsites; YARA cannot distinguish, so use a per-target triage (re-triage) to validate.
Compilers may emit LLDT/LTR (0F 00 /0 and /1) for switch/case in some patterns -- these are not the red-pill, they are integer-to-LEA.

offender.summary: Virtualize the IDT base in the VMM (Hyper-V, KVM) so the user-mode SIDT returns 0x80xxxx. For VBox/VMware/VirtualBox, the IDT base is set on VMX-VMCS-write and must be intercepted on the host side. Use re-anti-vm-spoof's frida hook to overwrite the SIDT return value at the call site.
limitations: The IDT base is the host IDT base, set by the VMM. Some VMMs (QEMU/KVM with kernel-active patches) can scrub it; others (older VBox, plain VirtualBox) cannot.
Some protectors also check the GDT/IDT limit and flags -- must be scrubbed together.

references.path_or_id: Peter Ferrie, 'Attacks on More Virtual Machine Emulators' (2007)

Driven by F1 25 Iconic Edition (InsaneRamZes crack) — 23 VMCALL + 2.18M CPUID anti-VM primitives.

defender.summary: F1 25 (InsaneRamZes crack) imports wine_get_version directly. EA SPEAR AntiCheat's anti-VM layer also probes Wine via the wine_get_version and wine_get_build_id exports from ntdll.dll (Wine provides these; Windows does not). GetModuleHandle('ntdll.dll') + GetProcAddress lookup is the canonical pattern. EA AC escalates to kernel-mode on positive match.

detection_signatures.note: Universal DLL -- pair with one of the wine_* strings

false_positive_risks: Cross-platform compat shims (Proton, Wine wrappers) may also import these. Pair with the AC escalation behavior (kernel-mode driver load after positive match).

offender.summary: The cleanest mitigation: don't run the protected binary under Wine. The cracked F1 25 refuses to start under Wine by design (the AC layer escalates). For legitimate research, use re-anti-vm-spoof's frida hook to return NULL from GetProcAddress(ntdll, 'wine_get_version') and the binary will believe it's on Windows.
limitations: The probe may be a runtime GetModuleHandle('kernel32.dll') + GetProcAddress('wine_get_version') combo (no static import). The frida hook must intercept GetProcAddress, not just the wine_get_version entry point.

references.note: F1 25 EAAntiCheat.Installer.exe + F1_25.exe both import wine_get_version.

entries.name: EA SPEAR AntiCheat (EAAntiCheat.GameServiceLauncher + EAAntiCheat.Installer + antitamperdiagnosis endpoint)

defender.summary: EA's in-house anti-cheat (built by the EA SPEAR AntiCheat Engineering team, code-signed with OU 'EA SPEAR AntiCheat Engineering'). Distinct from EasyAntiCheat (BattlEye) and Denuvo Anti-Cheat. Ships with every EA Sports title since FC 24 + F1 23. The GameServiceLauncher.exe + GameServiceLauncher.dll pair is the kernel-mode driver + service entry point. The /antitamperdiagnosis HTTP endpoint is the AC's health-check / heartbeat.

detection_signatures.note: Cert OU -- present in the EAAntiCheat binaries, NOT the game .exe

false_positive_risks: Low. The 'EAAntiCheat' string is unique to EA's AC; no other vendor uses it.

offender.summary: EA AC's anti-VM layer is centered on wine_get_version + the antitamperdiagnosis endpoint. The 23 VMCALL + 2.18M CPUID counts in F1 25 (cracked) are the kernel-mode AC probe layer. For the .bak (original) the counts are 0 VMCALL + 281 CPUID. Use re-anti-vm-spoof to spoof wine_get_version -> return NULL. The AC will refuse to escalate to kernel-mode without a Wine positive.
limitations: EA AC also calls the Denuvo ATD's heartbeat -- must spoof both.

references.note: F1 25 EAAntiCheat.Installer.exe (251MB, 8 sections, kernel-active posture) -- same modification timestamp as F1_25.exe.

entries.name: InsaneRamZes crack-time preloader (preloader_l.dll, /work/preloader.pdb, full process-injection surface)

defender.summary: The InsaneRamZes group ships a small (~46 KB) preloader DLL named preloader_l.dll with every cracked EA title. Built on a Linux CI runner (PDB path /work/preloader.pdb, single export preloader_link_func), it imports the full Win32 process-injection surface: NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, NtProtectVirtualMemory, ZwCreateUserProcess, ZwCreateThreadEx, plus LdrAccessResource / LdrFindEntryForAddress / LdrGetProcedureAddress for module traversal. The preloader launches the host .exe in a suspended state, injects the patched binary, and the anti-cheat sees only the patched binary. This is the smoking gun for any InsaneRamZes cracked release.

detection_signatures.note: Universal InsaneRamZes preloader PDB path -- per-build signature
Preloader source artifact name
Single export of the preloader
Process-injection import surface
Size + section-count window -- preloader is 30-80KB, 5-7 sections

false_positive_risks: Generic process-injection helpers (custom loaders, debug tools) match the import set. The /work/preloader.pdb path is the unique signature -- keep that as the primary, demote the others.

offender.summary: Hash-list the preloader. Add the SHA-256 of preloader_l.dll (and any rebuild) to the AC's blocked-modules list. Alternatively, the AppInit_DLLs registry key can be set to load an anti-preloader DLL that blocks the preloader from running. The defender has the advantage: the preloader is a per-build static binary, while the protected game changes with every patch.
limitations: The preloader changes per build (different SHA-256) -- keep the hash list updated.

references.note: F1 25 preloader_l.dll (46,840 bytes, 6 sections, single export, full process-injection import set).

entries.name: Denuvo ATD + EA SPEAR AntiCheat + Wine detection joint surface (F1 25 / FC 25 / future EA SPORTS)

defender.summary: F1 25 Iconic Edition (and FC 25, FC 24) ship with BOTH Denuvo ATD (denuvo_atd section + Denuvo GmbH cert) AND EA SPEAR AntiCheat (EAAntiCheat.GameServiceLauncher). The .exe itself imports wine_get_version for Wine detection. This is a unique joint surface -- the Denuvo + EAC + Wine triple is only seen in EA SPORTS titles since F1 23. Defenders should not assume Denuvo-only or EAC-only signatures; the joint match is the high-confidence rule.

detection_signatures.note: Generic; pair with EAAntiCheat to confirm EA SPORTS
F1 25 has the lowercase 'denuvo_atd' string (not 'Denuvo' mixed case)
Generic; pair with Denuvo for joint match
F1 25 + FC 25 .exe import this; pair with the above
Denuvo ATD section set

false_positive_risks: Low. The joint (Denuvo + EAAntiCheat + wine_get_version) is unique to EA SPORTS titles.

offender.summary: The joint surface requires spoofing all three layers: Denuvo (the vfs, separate playbook), EAC (wine_get_version -> NULL), and the Wine import. The Denuvo bypass is the hardest (months of per-title work); the EAC layer is medium; the Wine layer is trivial.
limitations: Denuvo's online license check is the gating factor -- even with the ATD + EAC bypassed, the binary must phone home to Denuvo's server.
EA SPORTS titles update aggressively; per-build breakage is common.

references.note: F1 25 Iconic Edition: 23 sections, denuvo_atd section, EAAntiCheat binaries, wine_get_version import, +23 VMCALL + 2.18M CPUID anti-VM layer.
